title: The Eye That Must Become Sunlike — Plotinus and the Vision of Beauty
author: Plotinus

body.text: Plotinus begins from beauty — the trace by which the soul remembers its origin. A beautiful body, action, or thought awakens a hidden kinship between the soul and the intelligible world. The visible becomes a ladder. Beauty draws the soul inward and upward until perception itself is purified.
When you know that you have become this perfect work, strain and see.

Plotinus tells the reader to become capable of seeing. The eye must be changed before the vision can occur. The imaginal is accessed by refining the perceiver — the object may be luminous, but the eye must become adequate to it. The soul must call itself back from dispersion until it is gathered enough to see.

body.caption: The soul as hidden statue — uncovered by purification, not built by addition.

body.text: This is the only eye that sees, having some likeness to it.

Likeness is the condition of perception — the eye must become sunlike to see the sun. The soul must become beautiful to behold beauty. This is the Platonic law that governs all imaginal work. The perceiver and the perceived must share a nature. The work is carving — cutting away what is excessive until the hidden form appears.
Withdraw into yourself and look. Cut away all that is excessive, straighten all that is crooked, bring light to what is dark.

Withdraw. Look. Cut. Straighten. Lighten. The verbs are practical instructions for imaginal practice — the soul is marble containing a divine form. The work is removal. When the carver is finished, the soul sees because it has become what it sought.

body.caption: The sunlike eye — the perceiver transformed into likeness with what it beholds.

body.text: Multiply no images. Purify the single eye. When the soul is gathered, simple, and self-gathered, it sees by kinship what it could not see by effort. The vision arrives as a reward — it arrives as the natural perception of a being that has become like what it loves.
